risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "A computer-generated randomization table was created by the research pharmacist and used to randomize participants." Comment: This explicitly describes a proper random sequence generation method (computer-generated randomization), which supports a Low risk judgement for random sequence generation.

Quote: "Participants underwent a comprehensive medical evaluation, baseline assessments and were randomized by the pharmacy in a one-to-one fashion." and "A computer-generated randomization table was created by the research pharmacist and used to randomize participants." Comment: The study explicitly states that randomization was handled by the pharmacy/pharmacist, which is a mechanism that can prevent the enrolling clinician from foreseeing the next assignment. Although the paper does not use the exact phrase "central allocation," pharmacy-controlled randomization is an explicit concealment mechanism and is sufficient here to judge low risk for allocation concealment.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "This was a randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled, parallel design trial of IN-OXT versus placebo in adults with ASD." Comment: The study explicitly states it was double-blind and placebo-controlled, and further notes that "Placebo was normal saline in identical bottles and labels," indicating both participants and personnel were intended to be blinded. Because the intervention was intranasal oxytocin and the outcomes included subjective/behavioral measures that could be affected by knowledge of allocation, the explicit double-blind, placebo-matched design supports a low risk judgement for performance bias.

Quote: "All efficacy assessments were carried out by an independent evaluator who was blinded to both side effects and group assignment. All safety evaluations were performed by a physician who was blinded to group assignment. Although we are not aware of any characteristic side effects of IN-OXT, this approach assured that all efficacy assessments were done by a truly blinded clinician." Comment: The study explicitly states that outcome assessment was performed by blinded assessors. The main efficacy outcomes included clinician-rated measures (e.g., CGI-improvement) and administered tests, and the paper indicates the evaluator was blinded to allocation, so knowledge of assignment was unlikely to influence outcome measurement.

Quote: "Sixteen of the 19 patients (84%) had data at week 6. For the remaining three subjects, we imputed week 6 ratings using expectation-maximization (EM) methods and the earlier CGI ratings." Comment: There was substantial missing outcome data at the main endpoint (3/19 = 15.8% overall), with complete-case data available for only 16 participants. More importantly, the paper reports early terminations in both arms, including "one participant with stable epilepsy... was terminated for caution at week 4" and "There were two early terminations due to worsening tics (week 4) and a panic attack..." Because the study used imputation for missing CGI values and mixed-effects/FIML for other outcomes, but attrition was still non-trivial and one dropout reason (worsening tics) is plausibly related to outcome/tolerability, this is not low risk. Given the imbalance/clinical relevance of withdrawals and the small sample size, attrition could bias results.
